name: TypeScript SaaS
description: Conventions for a typical TypeScript SaaS team building API-driven products on Next.js + Postgres. Covers API shape, error handling, logging, auth, deployment, and test discipline. Founder-curated — review before merging into your project's decision base.

decisions.summary: All API responses return JSON with a top-level `data` or `error` key — never bare values
decisions.rationale: Frontend and SDK code branches on response shape. A consistent envelope means error handling is uniform across services and we never have to teach a new caller "oh, this endpoint returns an array, but that one returns an object." Errors carry `{error: '<code>', message: '<human>'}` so consumers can switch on the code without parsing free text.

decisions.summary: API errors return structured codes — never substring-match on user-visible messages
decisions.rationale: Error messages are for humans and may be reworded for clarity or i18n at any time without a contract change. The `error` field is the machine-readable contract. Tests asserting on message strings are flaky — switch to asserting on `error` codes.

decisions.summary: Use `pino` (or equivalent structured logger) — never `console.log` in production paths
decisions.rationale: Production logs go to a structured aggregator (CloudWatch / Datadog / Honeycomb). `console.log` produces unparseable strings that cost queryability. Use the structured logger with named fields so every log line is filterable.

decisions.summary: Bcrypt cost factor must be ≥ 12 for password hashing
decisions.rationale: Cost 12 takes ~250ms on modern hardware — a sweet spot between user-visible login latency and brute-force resistance. Higher costs (14+) are reserved for highly sensitive use cases. Never use raw SHA-* for passwords.

decisions.summary: JWT access tokens TTL ≤ 15 minutes; refresh tokens with rotation on every use
decisions.rationale: Short access TTL minimizes blast radius if a token leaks. Refresh-token rotation means a leaked refresh token is invalidated on the legitimate user's next call. Stolen tokens have a hard cap on usefulness.

decisions.summary: All PII stored in Postgres uses column-level encryption via pgcrypto or equivalent
decisions.rationale: Plaintext PII in the database is a regulatory and breach-impact risk. Encrypted columns can still be queried for equality (with deterministic encryption) or for aggregate counts (without revealing values). Application-layer keys live in a managed secret store, not env vars in source.

decisions.summary: Database migrations are forward-only — no destructive rollback in production
decisions.rationale: Rolling back a migration that already changed data is a worse outcome than rolling forward with a fix-up migration. We accept that one-way migrations require more discipline at write time and deliver more reliable production behavior.

decisions.summary: Schema changes are additive — drop columns only after a deprecation cycle
decisions.rationale: Adding a column is safe and zero-downtime. Dropping or retyping is not. Standard cycle: add new column → backfill → switch readers → switch writers → drop old column in a separate release.

decisions.summary: All cron jobs and background workers must be idempotent
decisions.rationale: Retries are a fact of life with at-least-once delivery. A job that doubles its effect when it retries (sending two emails, charging twice, applying a delta twice) is a production incident waiting to happen. Idempotency keys + upserts are the discipline.

decisions.summary: Rate-limit every public-facing endpoint; default deny for unauthenticated traffic
decisions.rationale: Unrate-limited public endpoints are an outage vector and a cost-leak vector. Authenticated traffic gets per-user limits; unauthenticated traffic gets per-IP limits. Default deny means the burden of proof is on the developer to justify why a route is open.

decisions.summary: Use ULID or UUIDv7 for entity IDs — never auto-increment integers
decisions.rationale: Time-sortable, globally unique, no synchronization needed across shards or services. Predictable integer IDs are an info-leak (`/users/1` vs `/users/1000` reveals how big you are) and break in distributed-write topologies.

decisions.summary: Every state-changing operation writes a row to `audit_entries` — no exceptions
decisions.rationale: Compliance, debugging, and support all rely on a complete audit trail. The cost of an audit row at write time is microseconds; the cost of NOT having one when an investigation lands is hours. Audit row carries who/what/when/why.

decisions.summary: Tests targeting external APIs use mocks at the HTTP layer (vi.stubGlobal('fetch'))
decisions.rationale: Mocking at the application-code layer (e.g. mocking the Stripe client) hides real bugs in request shape. Mocking at fetch lets the real client + real serializers run and only stubs the network. This is what production actually does — modulo the network being real.

decisions.summary: Integration tests run against a real Postgres (not a mock) with truncate-between-tests
decisions.rationale: Mocked DB tests pass when the migration is broken. We've been bitten — keep integration tests honest by running them against the real schema. Truncate between tests for isolation; transactions are not enough when we want to test cross-transaction visibility.

decisions.summary: All env vars are typed at the boundary; never `process.env.X` deep in business logic
decisions.rationale: Reading raw `process.env` from any file is a coupling to deployment configuration. Centralize in a `config.ts` boundary that parses + validates env at startup. A missing var crashes startup loudly instead of silently producing wrong behavior at the first use site.

decisions.summary: Feature flags via a stable provider (LaunchDarkly, Statsig, GrowthBook); never hardcoded `if (env === 'prod')`
decisions.rationale: Hardcoded environment gates couple feature visibility to deployment, making rollback hard and per-user gating impossible. A flag service decouples "who sees it" from "is it deployed." Default-off, kill-switchable, observable.

decisions.summary: Public-facing pages render on the server (SSR/RSC) by default; client components are an opt-in
decisions.rationale: SSR-first means SEO + first-paint + zero JS for static content. Client components are reserved for genuine interactivity (forms, real-time updates). The boundary is explicit via `'use client'` directives.

decisions.summary: Every PR includes a test for the change — no untested merges to main
decisions.rationale: Regression prevention. Code without tests is unsupported by anyone but its author. The discipline is: "the test you write today is the regression you don't pay for in 6 months." CI enforces that test count moves forward, not backward.
